name: Thorn's Departure
description: Thorn has made his decision. He knows about the reversal. He chose not to take it. He has three days left at the post. This is the last conversation.
accept_text: Thorn is at the maps when you arrive, same as always. But something is different — the red ink corrections from this week are lighter, smaller. He's been working at half capacity and not hiding it, which means he's done hiding things. He looks up when you enter. 'I know why you're here,' he says. 'Stone told me about the Hall. The Ninth, the technique, the documentation.' He sets down the pen. 'I told her I needed three days.' A pause. 'I've had the three days.' He looks at the crystal growth along his forearm — the slow blue that has been advancing since the third application. 'My list of objections is long. The first one is: I'm not sure what I'm being offered is me, at the end of it. The second one is: the crystal has been in me for three years and I don't know what I am without it anymore.' He meets your eyes. 'The third one is that the man who went in for the third application knew what he was doing and made a choice, and I'm not certain I have the right to undo his choice on the grounds that I'm afraid of the ending.' He stands up. 'I told Serel last month. In a letter. She replied in a day, which is not her pace — she writes slowly and thinks longer. One line.' He looks at the wall where the maps hang. 'She said: whatever you decide, decide it as you, not as the version of you that thinks it doesn't deserve to stay.' He is quiet for a moment. 'I've been thinking about what version of me makes the decision. And I think — I think the version of me that says no to the reversal is afraid, and the version of me that says yes is also afraid, and neither of them is wrong to be.' He picks up his coat. 'I'm going east. I want to see what's out there before I run out of time to see it. The post will be fine — Desh has been running the daily operations since last winter.' He pauses. 'Three days. Come back before dawn on the third day.'
complete_text: Dawn on the third day. Thorn is at the command post door with a pack — small, a soldier's kit, nothing unnecessary. The red-ink maps are still on the table. He left them. The command post looks like someone has been working in it recently and also like someone has said goodbye to it, and those two things are usually opposites but aren't, here. He has Serel's letter in his breast pocket. You can see the outline of the folded paper. He doesn't explain it and you don't ask. 'I want to ask you something,' he says. He looks east, toward the Wastes, toward whatever is out there that he's decided he would rather walk into than wait for in a room. 'I've been asking whether what I spent my life doing was worth doing. Standard question for someone in my situation. The kind that doesn't get answered.' He looks at you. 'I'm not going to ask it. I've decided it doesn't matter — not because it wasn't worth asking, but because I've been answering it for two years and the answer is always the same and the answer is: I don't know. And I've decided that I don't know is sufficient.' He holds out his hand. 'Tell Adra I didn't come back wrong. Tell Desh the supply rotation shifts to the Aetherian schedule in the spring — we worked that out during the armistice last year and I didn't get to write it up.' He pauses. 'Tell Serel —' He stops. 'No. I'll tell Serel.' He lets go of your hand. He walks east. The morning is clear, cold, the Wastes grey and flat in the early light. He walks without looking back, which is very Thorn. You watch until he's a small figure, then a very small figure, then the Wastes.
